text: Robotic transanal fistula repair is a technique advanced robotic surgeons should possess in their armamentarium. The motion and degrees of freedom are unmatched compared with standard transanal surgery. This allows more complex surgery to be performed in a minimally invasive fashion.

We present a case of a fifty-nine-year-old female with a history of diverticulitis complicated with a stricture in her right ureter. During her robotic sigmoid colectomy, the right ureter was intentionally divided and reimplanted into the bladder by urology. Two months after her ileostomy was reversed, she started experiencing back and flank pain. CT demonstrated air tracking to the right kidney from a colonic ureteral fistula.

The CT scan slice shows air present in the right kidney and bladder. During flexible sigmoidoscopy and cystoscopy, a fistula is identified from the colorectal anastomosis to the distal ureteral stump, allowing air to extravasate up the re-implanted ureter to the right kidney.

The surgical team sets up the robotic port configuration, placing the transanal port while the patient is positioned in the prone jackknife position. A rigid proctoscopy reveals the fistula opening at seventeen centimeters, and the robot is docked parallel to the operating room table.
The surgeon identifies the lesion and uses a 2-0 absorbable self-locking suture to oversew it. The suturing is performed in a running fashion until the fistula opening is completely closed.
The patient is positioned in lithotomy, and a cystoscopy is performed. The surgeon uses fibrin glue and electrocautery to obliterate the right ureteral stump and fistula.
The patient was started on a clear liquid diet and advanced as tolerated. She was discharged on post-op day number one.
Robotic surgery is useful when performing complex transanal procedures. The three hundred sixty degree range of motion, three dimensional views, and ergonomics greatly assist the surgeon.
